name.latinName: Allium × proliferum (hybrid of Allium cepa × Allium fistulosum)

range.nativeRange: Not applicable — cultivated hybrid; unknown in the wild; long cultivated across Europe and Asia

usNativeStatus.description: Not native to the US; an ancient cultivated hybrid; grown throughout the US; not naturalized aggressively in the wild

invasive.description: Not invasive in the US; spreads by topsets and division but is cultivated and not a documented invasive

zone.description: 3-9 — among the hardiest of all onion types

description.spread: 6-8" per clump; slowly expands and "walks" as topset bulbils weigh down the stalk and root where they touch the ground
description.spacing: 6-8" for ground cover; 12" for individual clumps
description.growthRate: Moderate; established plants are essentially permanent
description.timeToMaturity: Productive from first year; bulbils form in first season
description.lifespan: Essentially permanent — a truly perennial onion that requires no replanting; continues indefinitely once established
description.deciduousEvergreen: Semi-evergreen — green foliage often persists into winter in Zone 6a; dies back in the coldest periods; one of the first plants to produce harvestable green growth in late winter/early spring
description.winterInterest: Moderate — hollow green leaves often visible through winter; early green growth is a welcome sight in February-March

lightAndWater.sun: Full sun to part shade; more shade-tolerant than annual onions
lightAndWater.water: Low to medium; drought tolerant once established

drainagePreference.description: Average to well-drained; avoids waterlogged conditions

claySoilPerformance.description: Good; more tolerant of clay and wet conditions than most Allium species; the robust bulblets and fibrous root system handle clay better than true bulb onions; still benefits from drainage improvement but in-ground planting in clay is generally successful

soil.texturePreference: Adaptable; performs in average garden soil including clay-loam
soil.rootCharacter: True bulb at the base; produces hollow tubular leaves from the base; "topsetting" bulbils (clusters of small onion bulbs) form at the top of the flowering scape

bloom.bloomTime: June-July (scape formation); topset bulbils mature August-September
bloom.bloomDescription: Hollow green scapes (stems) topped with a cluster of small red-purple bulbils rather than flowers; the weight of the bulbil cluster causes the scape to arch and touch the ground, where bulbils root — this is the "walking" mechanism
bloom.fragrance: Strong onion scent from foliage and bulbils
bloom.flowerSex: Vegetatively reproducing — does not typically produce viable flowers; reproduces by topset bulbils

yield.edibleParts: Leaves (hollow green tops — mild onion flavor; use like chives or green onions throughout the growing season); underground bulbs (harvest in fall; small onion-flavored bulbs; use like shallots or pickling onions); topset bulbils (harvest when mature in late summer; strong flavor; excellent pickled or used fresh); scapes (tender young scapes edible)
yield.harvestSeason: Green tops year-round except deep winter; underground bulbs fall; bulbils late summer-fall
yield.medicinalUses: Same general antimicrobial and cardiovascular properties as other Allium species; primarily a culinary and productive garden plant
yield.craftOtherYield: Pickled topset bulbils (a traditional specialty — the small bulbils are excellent pickled in brine or vinegar)

cautionsAndHazards.toxicityAnimals: TOXIC to dogs and cats — all Allium species cause hemolytic anemia; never feed to dogs, cats, or horses; this is the standard Allium caution
cautionsAndHazards.allelopathy: Allium exudates deter pest insects and some soil pathogens — a positive companion planting effect

roles: Food (all parts edible throughout the year — exceptional year-round harvest potential)
Pest Confuser (allium exudates deter pests and pathogens)
companionPlants: Most vegetables and herbs; excellent throughout the food forest understory; plant around fruit trees
and other shrubs as a permanent understory element; pairs with garlic chives and chives for a multi-species allium guild planting
antagonists: Peas and beans — as with all alliums
avoid planting adjacent to legumes; does not pair well with Trifolium (clover) ground cover directly
permaculture.coppiceable: Cut green tops as needed throughout the season; the plant regrows from the base; clipping tops regularly keeps the plant tidy and productive

wildlife.attracts: Minimal direct wildlife value (the pungent scent deters most animals); when bulbils fall and the plant "walks," it can provide cover for ground-dwelling insects

deer.description: Excellent — all Allium species are strongly avoided by deer; one of the most reliable deer deterrents in the garden; planting as a border or interspersed through beds provides protective function for neighboring plants

drought.description: Excellent once established — one of the most drought-tolerant edible plants

tolerances.other: Extremely cold-hardy to Zone 3; overwinters without any protection in Zone 6a; one of the most low-maintenance productive plants available

maintenance.pruningNotes: Harvest green tops as needed throughout the season; collect topset bulbils in late summer for eating and replanting; if spread is not desired, collect all bulbils before they "walk" and root; divide underground clumps every 3-5 years to rejuvenate production
propagationMethods: Topset bulbils (the primary propagation method — plant bulbils 1-2" deep in fall or spring; they establish quickly); division of underground clumps in spring or fall

cultivarNotes: No widely marketed named cultivars; the species is typically sold as "Egyptian walking onion" or "tree onion." Various strains exist with different bulbil sizes and leaf widths — seek out strains with large, productive underground bulbs if that is the primary yield goal. Some strains have red-purple bulbils (more ornamental) vs. pale green-brown.

gardenUse.designPlacement: Perennial vegetable garden, food forest understory, herb garden edge

pennsylvaniaHegins.nativeToSite: Not native to PA; cultivated hybrid
pennsylvaniaHegins.drainage: Hegins red clay: in-ground planting generally acceptable; no raised bed required; avoid the most waterlogged spots
pennsylvaniaHegins.soil: Hegins red clay is tolerable for walking onions; in-ground planting works well
pennsylvaniaHegins.cultivarNotes: Walking onions are highly cold-hardy and perform excellently in Zone 6a; already established at Hegins
pennsylvaniaHegins.designPlacement: Already established at Hegins; Section B or food forest edge; low-maintenance perennial vegetable
pennsylvaniaHegins.siteObservations: Confirmed established on site. Note the walking habit — topset bulbils will root where the scape falls; allow or collect as management preference dictates.
